danger_signals.visual_form: Colored police vehicles that follow the player on-screen
danger_signals.meaning: Active threat requiring evasion or offensive action via oil cans

danger_signals.visual_form: Cat sprites with flags that block pathways
danger_signals.meaning: Static obstacles that cannot be killed; must be avoided or bypassed

danger_signals.visual_form: Spikes that extrude from the stage ground
danger_signals.meaning: Instant-death hazard triggered by prolonged stationary play

danger_signals.visual_form: Road sections that differ in color from painted sections
danger_signals.meaning: Incomplete objective state; player must traverse to change to green

safety_signals.visual_form: Road sections changed from white to green after traversal
safety_signals.meaning: Completed objective areas; visual progress indicator

safety_signals.visual_form: Red-colored collectible objects appearing occasionally in stages
safety_signals.meaning: Bonus items; three collected warps player to new area and grants points

safety_signals.visual_form: Police cars or traffic vehicles rendered immobile after oil can impact
safety_signals.meaning: Temporarily neutralized threats; safe to ram and remove from stage

faction_indicators.color_identity: Determined by stage/variant (not specified in sources)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Police cars that pursue and follow player; can be stunned but not permanently eliminated until rammed while stunned
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: None documented; police appear as mobile threats rather than occupying fixed locations

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Vehicle sprite; blue-haired female character (Famicom/arcade) or unnamed male (NES NA)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Trail of painted roads left behind as player drives; visual mark of player's presence and progress

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Cats are invulnerable and block pathways; traffic vehicles can be stunned like police
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Cats stationed at fixed points; traffic vehicles appear to patrol or cross paths

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: White unpainted roads dominate; few obstacles visible; open pathways
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Police cars patrol slowly or are distant; cats positioned at limited choke points

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Mix of white and green roads; more visual clutter; police cars positioned closer
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Police pursuit intensifies; multiple vehicles on-screen; cat placement increases

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Ground spikes emerge and extrude visually from stage floor
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Instant-death hazard forces player to either complete objective or lose life; urgency escalates

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: All roads turn green; visual confirmation of full traversal
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Progression to next stage; world shifts to new location backdrop

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Background shifts to iconic real-world location (New York, London, Paris, etc.)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Visual storytelling through environmental backdrops; twelve stages span globe; musical track changes per stage

environmental_storytelling.example: Big Ben and Tower Bridge for London stage; Arc de Triomphe and Eiffel Tower for Paris; Mount Fuji for Japan; Sydney Opera House for Australia

Tchaikovsky's Piano Concerto No. 1 used across multiple stages; 'Flohwalzer' (Neko Funjatta) plays when hitting flag-waving cats; Deep Purple's 'Highway Star' remix in another stage; credited as one of first games to use unique music per stage

Twelve stages depicting world tour: USA → UK → France → Germany → Netherlands → Egypt → India → China → Japan → Australia → Latin America → USA; player travels in Honda City hatchback on 'quest of finding herself the perfect man'
White roads convert to green as player drives over them; visual manifestation of objective completion and territorial control

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Follow player around stage; chase continuously; can be stunned by oil cans; knocked off stage when rammed while stunned
Rendered immobile and vulnerable to ramming; temporary neutralization lasting briefly
Remain fixed in position waving flags; cannot be killed or stunned by any means; force player to jump over or find alternate route
Function similarly to police cars; can be stunned by oil cans and rammed; appear to patrol or cross paths with player
Appear randomly in stage; collecting three triggers warp to new area and bonus points; incentivizes exploration and risk-taking
Extrude from ground after extended player stasis; instant-kill mechanic forcing player action and preventing idle camping
Drives horizontally across elevated roads; can jump over gaps to reach higher sections; collects and launches oil cans as offensive tool; leaves trail of painted roads

notes: City Connection operates as a chase-evasion action platformer with minimal political or factional narrative. World-state communication relies primarily on visual environmental changes (painted roads, spike emergence, police proximity) and location backdrops rather than complex political dynamics. The game's faction structure is simple: player vs. law enforcement vs. environmental hazards. Danger signals are performance-based (time pressure, proximity threats) rather than narrative. The global stage design and character journey theme provide light environmental storytelling but no explicit world-state politics. Data extracted from available Wikipedia and TCRF disambiguation sources; detailed game mechanics sourced from Wikipedia gameplay and development sections.
